title: 호감이 있어도 표현을 못 하는 사람의 2가지 특징
text: Have you ever wanted to approach someone but hesitated because you lacked the courage? In this 5-minute Level Up, based on two reference books, I will teach you two ways to take courageous action without hesitating out of fear. The first is distancing yourself. Can you do a backflip? You probably can't. That's because it's just a difficult move. However, approaching someone is such a simple movement, so why is it so hard? Oh, what did you spill today? Ah, I spilled some coffee while drinking it. Oh, I see. You should be more careful. Oh, then you can come pick it up tomorrow. Ah, ah, okay. Thank you. As humans, we can be drawn to someone and feel the urge to take a step closer. But... uh, by the way, there wasn't originally a number tag system, right? Hannah, okay. Then goodbye. Approaching someone you are attracted to is not as easy as you might think. Because if the other person feels uncomfortable, how can I be such a huge nuisance? Ah, no, I lack courage. The courage to get hurt. That is why you can't even take any action. However, did you know there is a way to immediately regain confidence and gain the courage to act in moments like this? That method is to say, "Yes, I can do it." Instead of referring to yourself in the first person, say, "Yes." " Wang-bae, you can do it." This is supporting them by using the third person. In fact, a team led by Professor Ethan Cross at the University of Michigan conducted an interesting experiment. First, they had participants deliver speeches; they asked Group A to use the first-person subject "I," while asking Group B to use their own name as the third-person subject. As a result, Group B delivered their speeches much better overall than Group A, reported feeling less anxious, and brain scans showed they maintained a much more stable state. In other words, simply using a speech style that treats oneself as a third party allowed one to gain the psychological distance necessary to regain composure. However, the important point here is that when referring to yourself in the third person—thinking, "Yeah, Wang-bae, you can do it"—it's best to keep it to yourself if possible. [ Music] Isn't

[ Music] Isn't that embarrassing? Oh, what? Oppa, why did you come so late? [Music] Second, a polite question. Do you know why people want to get closer to someone they like but ca n't bring themselves to approach them? It is because they are cautious, worried that the other person might feel uncomfortable. "There seem to be too many strange people these days." "I know, right?" " Oh, come to think of it, where do you live, Hye-jeong? Let's be comfortable." During conversations, there are times when somewhat personal questions are asked. However, asking for overly personal information when you are not yet close can be burdensome. Therefore, rather than asking "Where is your house?", it is better to use indirect questions like "Where is your house?". Because you can answer vaguely, "Oh, I live near Hogae Intersection," or "Then where do you live, Si-woo?" and then answer specifically, "I live in Unit 600, Building 31, Complex 8, right in front." This allows you to answer based on your own judgment, making it much more comfortable and less burdensome. "Oh, what? Is it okay to just give out personal information like that?" "Oh, yes. Well, you can sell it to China if you want. It's fine." " Oh, but do you have a younger sister?" "Yeah, I do, but she bothers people so much." "Why?" "Oh, Oppa, why did you come so late?" " No, I mean, at least you came." " Making Oppa a shuttle for this crappy keyring... this is seriously too much." "Oh, did n't you get a hefty paycheck?" "Are you a crazy guy?" "Oh, I am grateful for that." "Uh, what is this?" " We need a pretty plausible excuse to approach someone..." I create it, and that justification can become the catalyst for a new relationship to blossom. But why? What is it? No matter how good the justification is, ah no, if you don't say anything, if you don't express anything, no drama can begin.
